_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal curve sloping upward since longer maturities usually carry higher yields reflecting risk and time value. An inverted yield curve slopes downward, typically signaling a forthcoming recession. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.55% for June 2026, and the 10-year to 2-year (2s10s) spread is 0.41 percentage points, indicating the curve is positively sloped. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the spread was inverted below 0.0 points, marking the deepest inversion historically in this series.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread acts as a recession indicator because an inverted curve has preceded all modern US recessions by 6 to 18 months. The spread spent 2022 to 2024 inverted but has since re-steepened above zero to 0.41 points, showing an un-inversion in the curve. The New York Fed recession probability currently stands at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk. However, the yield curve often un-inverts (returns to a normal slope) shortly before a recession actually begins, complicating the interpretation of current signals.
notes.now: The current positively sloped curve with a 0.41 point 2s10s spread suggests the immediate recession signal from the yield curve has diminished relative to the inverted period 2022-2024. Meanwhile, the 31% recession probability indicates ongoing elevated economic risk. Together, these metrics suggest caution but do not provide definitive timing for economic downturns.
